1.synthesis:
The sons of the prophets tell Elisha that the place where they dwell with him is too small; let them go to the Jordan and build there, each a log for themselves to make a place for them to dwell (vv. 1–2). Elisha agrees; one of them urges him to come with them, and he does (vv. 3–4). As one is felling a log, the iron axe head falls into the water, and he cries: alas, my master! It was borrowed (v. 5). Elisha asks where it fell; cuts a stick, throws it in at the spot, and the iron floats (v. 6). He says: pick it up; the man stretches out his hand and takes it (v. 7).

The floating iron axe head is the most apparently trivial of Elisha’s miracles — a lost borrowed tool recovered by supernatural means. Its inclusion in the canon is not incidental. The prophetic community was building a larger dwelling place at Elisha’s encouragement; the community’s growth required more space, and the community was growing through a collective building project using borrowed tools. The man’s distress at losing the borrowed axe head — “it was borrowed” — reflects the Deuteronomic concern about borrowed property (Exod 22:14–15): if a borrowed animal or tool is damaged or lost, the borrower is liable. The miracle removes the liability and restores the man to right relationship with whoever lent the tool.

The simplicity of the miracle is its theological point. Elisha’s prophetic ministry does not reserve miraculous intervention for national crises or royal emergencies; it extends to the material needs of ordinary community life — a borrowed axe head lost in a river during a construction project. The God who parts the Jordan for prophetic succession is also the God who makes iron float for a prophet-in-training who accidentally dropped his borrowed tool. The range of divine attention extends from the cosmic to the domestic without diminishment.
1.historical_context: Iron was valuable in the ancient Near East and not always readily available; the sons of the prophets apparently could not afford to buy their own tools and were using borrowed equipment for the construction project. The concern that the tool was borrowed rather than owned explains the intensity of the distress — this was not just a lost personal possession but an obligation to someone outside the community. The stick thrown into the water and the iron floating is presented without any natural explanation; the narrator offers no mechanism beyond the prophetic act and YHWH’s power. The detail that Elisha asked “where did it fall?” before acting suggests that the miracle required specific information about the location — it was not a general blessing on the river but a particular intervention at a particular spot.
1.literary_structure: 2 Kings 6:1–7 is a brief interlude before the extended Aramean warfare narrative (vv. 8–33). Its placement is deliberate: the same prophetic community whose axe head is recovered in a trivial miracle will face an invading army in the following narrative, and Elisha’s response to both situations demonstrates the same divine attentiveness — whether to a lost axe head or to a besieging army. The contrast in scale serves the same theological point: YHWH’s prophetic presence is available for community needs across the entire range of scale. The floating iron also echoes the water-parting at the Jordan in chapter 2 — Elisha again transforms the natural behavior of matter through prophetic intervention at the water’s edge.
1.theological_application: The prophetic community’s expansion — outgrowing its space, building a larger place for community life — and the miracle that attended it model the principle that the building of the covenant community’s life is attended by divine provision at even the most mundane level. YHWH’s care for the borrowed axe head is YHWH’s care for the integrity and sustainability of the prophetic community that was expanding at his servant’s direction. Communities of faith that are growing under divine commission can expect that the problems of growth — including the apparently trivial ones — will be met by divine attentiveness. The man who cried “it was borrowed” was concerned not only about the lost tool but about the community’s integrity toward those outside it; YHWH’s restoration of the tool preserved that integrity.
1.christology: The floating iron axe head has been read in Christian tradition as an image of the cross: the wood that goes into the water and causes the heavy iron to float, representing the instrument that lifts up what has fallen and restores it to the hand that needs it. The typological reading is not required by the text but is consistent with the broader pattern of wood-and-water motifs in the Elisha narratives (the meal in the water, the stick in the Jordan). More directly, the miracle models the principle of Matthew 10:29–31 — “not one sparrow falls to the ground apart from your Father”; “you are of more value than many sparrows” — applied to the material domain. The God who notes every sparrow’s fall also notes every borrowed axe head’s fall, and the same sovereign attention that governs the cosmos governs the prophetic community’s borrowed tools.

8.synthesis:
The king of Aram is warring against Israel. Each time the king camps at a particular spot, Elisha warns the king of Israel not to pass that way; several times Israel avoids the trap (vv. 8–10). The Aramean king is furious, suspecting a traitor in his court, but a servant tells him: Elisha the prophet in Israel tells the king of Israel the words you speak in your bedroom (vv. 11–12). He sends a great army with horses and chariots to Dothan, where Elisha is, and they surround the city by night (vv. 13–14). Elisha’s servant rises early and sees the army surrounding them; he cries: alas, my master! What shall we do? Elisha says: do not be afraid, for those who are with us are more than those who are with them (vv. 15–16). He prays for the servant’s eyes to be opened; the servant sees the mountain full of horses and chariots of fire surrounding Elisha (v. 17).

As the Arameans come down, Elisha prays for them to be struck with blindness; they are (v. 18). He leads them to Samaria, telling them he will lead them to the man they seek; then prays for their eyes to be opened and they find themselves in Samaria with the king of Israel standing before them (vv. 19–20). The king of Israel asks: shall I strike them down? Elisha says: you shall not strike them down — would you strike down those whom you have taken captive with your sword and your bow? Set bread and water before them, feed them and let them go to their master (vv. 21–22). The king prepares a great feast; they eat and drink and go back to their master. The Aramean raids stop for a while (v. 23).

The invisible divine army that Elisha’s servant sees — horses and chariots of fire on the surrounding mountains — is the most explicit revelation of the spiritual reality behind the physical situation in the entire Old Testament. The servant’s panic and Elisha’s calm are not the responses of two people in the same situation; they are the responses of two people with different vision of the same reality. The prophetic prayer for opened eyes — which becomes the decisive intervention — transfers to the servant the vision that sustains the prophet. The subsequent feasting of the enemy army is the logical extension of seeing reality truly: those whom YHWH has delivered are not enemies to be killed but people to be fed and released.
8.historical_context: Dothan (modern Tell Dotha, southwest of Jenin) is in the hill country of Ephraim, a strategic location for both Israelite and Aramean military movements. The “horses and chariots of fire” surrounding Elisha echo the chariot and horses of fire in which Elijah was taken up (2:11) — these are the divine military forces of YHWH, analogous to the “angel of YHWH” that camped around those who fear him (Ps 34:7). The feasting of the captured army rather than executing them as prisoners of war is unusual and is justified by Elisha on the basis that they were captured by divine agency rather than military force: you did not take them with your sword, so you do not have the warrior’s right of disposal over them.
8.literary_structure: 2 Kings 6:8–23 is structured as a two-stage divine intervention: YHWH’s intelligence advantage against Aram (vv. 8–12) → YHWH’s military presence around Elisha (vv. 13–17) → YHWH’s blinding of the army (vv. 18–20) → feasting and release (vv. 21–23). The unit contains two prayer-fulfillment pairs: opened eyes for the servant (vv. 17) and blindness for the army (v. 18), creating a structural contrast between what the servant sees and what the army cannot see. The ending — “the Aramean raids stopped for a while” — transitions directly to the siege narrative of vv. 24–33, where the next and more severe Aramean pressure begins.
8.theological_application: Elisha’s response to the servant’s panic — “those who are with us are more than those who are with them” — followed by the prayer for opened eyes is the paradigm of prophetic calm under threat. The prophet does not deny the physical reality of the surrounding army; he asserts that there is a greater reality that the servant cannot yet see. The opened-eye prayer is not a request for hallucination but for accurate perception — for seeing what is actually there rather than only what is visible to natural vision. Communities facing apparently overwhelming opposition are invited to the same sequence: acknowledge the visible threat, assert the invisible reinforcement, pray for opened eyes, and respond to the enemy with the abundance that overturned expectations allow. The feasting of the enemy army is possible only because Elisha saw beyond the immediate military threat to the divine reality that surrounded it.
8.christology: The invisible army of horses and chariots of fire that surrounds Elisha on the mountain echoes the angelic armies that surround Christ in the Gethsemane scene: “Do you think that I cannot appeal to my Father, and he will at once send me more than twelve legions of angels?” (Matt 26:53). Both Elisha and Jesus are surrounded by invisible divine military force that could deliver them from human threat; both choose a different path — Elisha by praying blindness on the army and leading them to feasting, Jesus by refusing to call the angels and choosing the cup of suffering. The divine army that Elisha reveals as present around the prophet becomes, in Christ, the army that is deliberately not deployed so that the greater victory of the cross can be won. The opened eyes that see the horses of fire are the same opened eyes that, after the resurrection, recognize the risen Lord in the breaking of bread (Luke 24:31).

24.synthesis:
Afterward, Ben-hadad king of Aram assembled all his army and went up and besieged Samaria (v. 24). The siege produces famine of terrifying severity: a donkey’s head costs eighty pieces of silver; a quarter of a kab of dove’s dung, five pieces of silver (v. 25). As the king of Israel is walking on the wall, a woman cries out to him: save me! He says: if YHWH does not save you, where shall I save you from — from the threshing floor or the winepress? (vv. 26–27). He asks what is wrong; she tells him: this woman said to me, give your son so we may eat him today, and tomorrow we will eat my son. So we boiled my son and ate him, and I said to her: give your son so we may eat him — and she has hidden her son (vv. 28–29). The king hears the woman’s words and tears his clothes; the people see he is wearing sackcloth beneath the royal garb (v. 30). He swears: may God do so to me and more also, if the head of Elisha the son of Shaphat remains on his shoulders today (v. 31).

The siege cannibalism narrative is the most horrifying passage in the Elisha cycle. The covenant curses of Deuteronomy 28:53–57 had threatened that covenant violation would result in eating one’s own children during siege conditions; the narrative reports the exact fulfillment of those curses in the Samaria siege. The king’s response to the woman’s story is complex: he tears his clothes, revealing that he has been wearing sackcloth beneath the royal garments all along (private mourning while publicly wearing the royal role), and then he blames Elisha. His theological logic is revealed: YHWH has brought this calamity — therefore, kill YHWH’s prophet. It is the logic of blaming the messenger for the message.
24.historical_context: Siege warfare frequently produced famine conditions severe enough to generate the practices described. The donkey’s head, normally refused as unclean meat, and dove’s dung (possibly wild vegetation or actual dung used as fuel) at extravagant prices indicate complete breakdown of the normal food economy. The parallel account of siege cannibalism at the fall of Jerusalem in Lamentations 4:10 confirms that this represented a historically documented extreme. The king’s sackcloth under the royal garments suggests he had already been in mourning — possibly for the siege’s casualties, possibly as a private act of penitence — but had not translated private grief into public repentance. Ben-hadad’s identity in this passage is uncertain; he may be the same Ben-hadad as chapter 6’s or a later king of the same name.
24.literary_structure: 2 Kings 6:24–33 is the narrative descent from the feasting of the Aramean army (v. 23) to the cannibalism inside Samaria during the Aramean siege — a devastating irony. Those who showed hospitality to the blinded army are now eating their own children. The king’s blame of Elisha frames the next chapter’s oracle of reversal: the man who threatened to kill the prophet will receive from the prophet the word of abundance. The sackcloth under the royal garments is a narrative detail that humanizes the king while also suggesting that private piety without public covenant faithfulness produces exactly the situation he is now facing — the private mourning of a king who did not walk in covenant faithfulness openly.
24.theological_application: The king’s response to the cannibalism report — tearing his clothes, revealing the sackcloth, and threatening to kill Elisha — is the response of a leader who has reached the end of his own resources and is looking for someone to blame. His theological instinct is partially correct: YHWH has brought this calamity, as the covenant curses promised. But his response — kill the prophet — is the inversion of the appropriate response: return to YHWH with full repentance rather than eliminate the witness to YHWH’s word. Leaders in crisis who respond to divine judgment by attacking YHWH’s representatives — prophetic voices, covenant reminders, uncomfortable truth-tellers — are in the king’s position: correctly diagnosing the calamity’s source while choosing exactly the wrong response to it. The sackcloth under the royal robes suggests that private suffering does not automatically produce public repentance.
24.christology: The eating of children during the Samaria siege is the nadir of the covenant violation trajectory that began with Jeroboam’s golden calves. The covenant curse fulfilled in children consumed as food finds its anti-type in the Eucharistic feast: the community that once ate its children is now fed by the body and blood of the Son of God who gives himself to be consumed so that his people might live. “Unless you eat the flesh of the Son of Man and drink his blood, you have no life in you” (John 6:53) — the Eucharistic language of eating and drinking that John 6 develops is the inversion of siege cannibalism: not the desperate consumption of children to survive, but the life-giving reception of the Son who offers himself freely that the community might not perish. The horror of the Samaria siege is the shadow; the Supper is the light that defines it.
